Syntax: summary-address <IPv6-ADDR>/<MASK> [no-advertise | tag <TAG-VALUE>]
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the transit-delay <DELAY> command do?

The transit-delay <DELAY> command sets the time delay in Link state transmission for virtual links. The no form of this command sets the delay in Link state transmission to the default of 1 second for virtual links. Enables the notification of the events to be sent as traps to the SNMP management stations for OSPFv3. The no form of this command disables the notification of the events to be sent as traps to the SNMP management stations for OSPFv3. Syntax: transit-delay <DELAY>.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the transit-delay <DELAY> command?
Syntax: transit-delay <DELAY>
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the address-family {IPv4 | IPv6} unicast command do?

The address-family {IPv4 | IPv6} unicast command specifies address family to use and changes to the configuration context for the specified family: • config-bgp-IPv4 for IPv4 • config-bgp-IPv6 for IPv6 • config-bgp-IPv4-uc for IPv4 unicast • config-bgp-IPv6-uc for IPv6 unicast The no form of this command removes the specified address family configuration. Syntax: address-family {IPv4 | IPv6} unicast.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the address-family {IPv4 | IPv6} unicast command?
Syntax: address-family {IPv4 | IPv6} unicast
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the aggregate-address <IP-ADDR>/<MASK> [as-set] [summary-only] command do?

The aggregate-address <IP-ADDR>/<MASK> [as-set] [summary-only] command creates an aggregate address entry in the BGP routing table. The no form of this command removes the specified aggregate address entry. Syntax: aggregate-address <IP-ADDR>/<MASK> [as-set] [summary-only].
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the aggregate-address <IP-ADDR>/<MASK> [as-set] [summary-only] command?
Syntax: aggregate-address <IP-ADDR>/<MASK> [as-set] [summary-only]
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the bgp always-compare-med command do?

The bgp always-compare-med command enables comparison of the Multi Exit Discriminator (MED) for paths from neighbors in different autonomous systems. Any changes in BGP configuration are applied by restarting the current BGP sessions on the VRFs. The no form of this command sets comparison of MED to the default setting (disabled). Syntax: bgp always-compare-med.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the bgp always-compare-med command?
Syntax: bgp always-compare-med
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the bgp asnotation dotted command do?

The bgp asnotation dotted command specifies that Autonomous System (AS) numbers greater than 65535 be shown in dotted integer format for all show commands, including running-configuration. The no form of this command restores the default format of non-dotted, simple integer. Specifies that all Autonomous System (AS) numbers be shown in dotted integer format for all show commands, including running-configuration. The no form of this command restores the default format of non-dotted, simple integer. Syntax: bgp asnotation dotted.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the bgp asnotation dotted command?
Syntax: bgp asnotation dotted
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the bgp bestpath as-path ignore command do?

The bgp bestpath as-path ignore command configures BGP to avoid considering the autonomous system (AS) path during best path route selection. By default, the AS-path is considered during BGP best path selection. Any changes in BGP configuration are applied by restarting the current BGP sessions on the VRFs. The no form of this command restores default behavior which configures BGP to consider the AS-path during route selection. Configures Border Gateway Protocol (BGP) to treat two BGP routes as equal cost even if their AS-paths differ, as long as their AS-path lengths and other relevant attributes are the same. This allows routes with different AS-paths to be programmed into the forwarding table as equal cost multipath routes. Any changes in BGP configuration are applied by restarting the current BGP sessions on the VRFs. Syntax: bgp bestpath as-path ignore.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the bgp bestpath as-path ignore command?
Syntax: bgp bestpath as-path ignore
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the bgp bestpath compare-routerid command do?

The bgp bestpath compare-routerid command configures a BGP routing process to compare identical routes received from different external peers during the best path selection process and selects the route with the lowest router ID as the best path. Defaults to disabled. Any changes in BGP configuration are applied by restarting the current BGP sessions in the VRFs. The no form of this command returns the BGP routing process to the default operation. By default, BGP selects the route that was received first when two routes with identical attributes are received. Syntax: bgp bestpath compare-routerid.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the bgp bestpath compare-routerid command?
Syntax: bgp bestpath compare-routerid
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the bgp bestpath med confed command do?

The bgp bestpath med confed command compares the identical routes received from the different confederation peers and selects the route with the lowest Multi Exit Discriminator (MED) value as the best path. This behavior is disabled by default. The no form of this command prevents the routing process from considering the MED value. NOTE: The selection of other attributes like as-multi-path relax and as-path ignore will not affect the behavior of this command within a confederation. Configures a BGP routing process to assign a value of infinity (max possible) to routes that are missing the Multi Exit Discriminator (MED) attribute. The path without a MED value is the least desirable path. Any changes in BGP configuration are applied by restarting the current BGP sessions in the VRFs. Syntax: bgp bestpath med confed.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the bgp bestpath med confed command?
Syntax: bgp bestpath med confed
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the bgp cluster-id {<IPv4-ADDR> | <ID>} command?
Syntax: bgp cluster-id {<IPv4-ADDR> | <ID>}
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the bgp confederation <AS-NUMBER> command do?

The bgp confederation <AS-NUMBER> command configures a BGP confederation with the confederation identifier. The group of Autonomous Systems (ASs) will be presented as a single autonomous system with the confederation identifier as the AS number. The no form of the command deletes the BGP confederation identifier. Configures BGP confederation peers with both same and different sub-autonomous system to establish an eBGP membership. You can configure a list of AS numbers separated by spaces. The no form of this command disables the peer session and deletes the peer information. Syntax: bgp confederation <AS-NUMBER>.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the bgp confederation <AS-NUMBER> command?
Syntax: bgp confederation <AS-NUMBER>
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the bgp default local-preference <NUMBER> command do?

The bgp default local-preference <NUMBER> command default local preference value for BGP learned routes. Any changes in BGP configuration are applied by restarting the current BGP sessions on the VRFs. The no form of this command sets the local preference to the default value of 100. Syntax: bgp default local-preference <NUMBER>.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the bgp default local-preference <NUMBER> command?
Syntax: bgp default local-preference <NUMBER>
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the bgp deterministric-med command do?

The bgp deterministric-med command enables comparison of the Multi-Exit Discriminator (MED) attribute when selecting routes advertised by different peers in the same autonomous system. Any changes in BGP configuration are applied by restarting the current BGP sessions on the VRFs. The no form of this command sets MED comparison to the default setting of disabled. Sets the switch to reset the BGP sessions of any directly adjacent external peers if the link used to reach them goes down. The no form of this command disables session reset. Syntax: bgp deterministric-med.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the bgp deterministric-med command?
Syntax: bgp deterministric-med
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the bgp graceful-restart restart-time <DELAY> command do?

The bgp graceful-restart restart-time <DELAY> command sets the graceful restart timer which determines how long the switch waits for a graceful-restart capable neighbor to re-establish BGP peering. Any changes in BGP configuration are applied by restarting the current BGP sessions on the VRFs. The no form of this command resets to the default value of 120 seconds. Syntax: bgp graceful-restart restart-time <DELAY>.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the bgp graceful-restart restart-time <DELAY> command?
Syntax: bgp graceful-restart restart-time <DELAY>
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the bgp log-neighbor-changes command do?

The bgp log-neighbor-changes command sets the stale path timer. This timer determines how long BGP keeps stale routes from the restarting BGP peer. Any changes in BGP configuration are applied by restarting the current BGP sessions on the VRFs. The no form of this command resets to the stale path timer to the default of 300 seconds. Enables logging of BGP neighbor session state changes. The no form of this command disables logging of changes in BGP neighbor adjacencies. Syntax: bgp log-neighbor-changes.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the bgp log-neighbor-changes command?
Syntax: bgp log-neighbor-changes
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the bgp maxas-limit <LENGTH> command do?

The bgp maxas-limit <LENGTH> command specifies the maximum size of AS paths in update messages. Routes with AS paths greater than the specified length are discarded. The no form of this command sets the limit to the default of 32. Configures a fixed router ID for the BGP peer process running on the router. Any changes in BGP configuration are applied by restarting the current BGP sessions on the VRFs. The no form of this command removes the fixed router ID from the running configuration and restores the default router ID selection. Syntax: bgp maxas-limit <LENGTH>.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the bgp maxas-limit <LENGTH> command?
Syntax: bgp maxas-limit <LENGTH>
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the clear bgp command do?

The clear bgp command resets BGP peer sessions. Sends a route refresh request when you have specified soft in. Optionally, you can specify reset for a specific VRF. Syntax: clear bgp [vrf <VRF-NAME>][IPv4 unicast | IPv6 unicast | l2vpn evpn | all].

syntax: clear bgp [vrf <VRF-NAME>][IPv4 unicast | IPv6 unicast | l2vpn evpn | all]

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the clear bgp command?
Syntax: clear bgp [vrf <VRF-NAME>][IPv4 unicast | IPv6 unicast | l2vpn evpn | all]

syntax: clear bgp [vrf <VRF-NAME>][IPv4 unicast | IPv6 unicast | l2vpn evpn | all]

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the disable | enable command do?

The disable | enable command this command disables or enables the BGP instance while retaining the configuration. Disable and enable of the BGP instance may result in a change of the router ID. By default the BGP instance is enabled. Configures the administrative distance for BGP. The no form of this command restores the default settings, 20 for eBGP and 200 for iBGP,. Syntax: disable | enable.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the disable | enable command?
Syntax: disable | enable
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the maximum-paths <MAXPATHS> command do?

The maximum-paths <MAXPATHS> command configures the maximum number of paths that BGP adds to the route table for equal-cost multipath (ECMP) load balancing for routes learned from both internal and external BGP. Any changes in BGP configuration are applied by restarting the current BGP sessions on the VRFs. The no form of this command restores the default setting of 4. Syntax: maximum-paths <MAXPATHS>.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the maximum-paths <MAXPATHS> command?
Syntax: maximum-paths <MAXPATHS>
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the neighbor activate command do?

The neighbor activate command this command enables the address-family capability and exchange of information specific to an address family with a BGP neighbor. The no form of this command removes the address-family capability and disables the exchange of routes for the specified address-family with the BGP neighbor. Syntax: neighbor <IP-ADDR> activate.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the neighbor activate command?
Syntax: neighbor <IP-ADDR> activate
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the neighbor advertisement-interval command do?

The neighbor advertisement-interval command sets the advertisement interval, which defines the length of time between transmission of BGP routing updates. The no form of this command restores the default value. Default values are 30 seconds for external BGP peer and 5 seconds for internal BGP peer. 204. Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDRESS>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} advertisement-interval <INTERVAL>.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the neighbor advertisement-interval command?
Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDRESS>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} advertisement-interval <INTERVAL>
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the neighbor add-paths command do?

The neighbor add-paths command enables additional path capability for BGP as described in RFC 7911. This allows BGP peer to send, receive, or send and receive multiple paths for the same address prefix without the subsequent advertisements implicitly replacing any previous paths. The additional path includes the first (N-1) best paths, which means that the total paths for an address prefix received by a BGP speaker will include the best path and the additional paths determined by its BGP peer. With additional path feature, each path is identified by a path identifier in addition to the address prefix. To use this command, the backup path of BGP next-hop must be different than the primary path. The no form of this command disables the additional path feature. Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} add-paths {send | recv | both}.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the neighbor add-paths command?
Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} add-paths {send | recv | both}
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the neighbor add-paths advertise-best command?
Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} add-paths advertise-best <2-4>
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the neighbor allowas-in command?
Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDRESS> | <PEER-GROUP-NAME>} allowas-in <LIMIT>
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the neighbor capability orf prefix-list command do?

The neighbor capability orf prefix-list command enables the Outbound Route filtering (ORF) capability with the neighbor in one of the three available modes. The available modes are send, receive, and both. The ORF capability is executed based on prefix list only. The Outbound Route Filtering (ORF) capability provides a mechanism for a BGP speaker to send a set of Outbound Route Filters (ORFs) that can be used by its BGP peer to filter its outbound routing updates to the speaker. This is a filtering method used to reduce the computation on the router receiving the route. The no form of this command disables the ORF capability. Syntax: neighbor <IP-ADDRESS> capability orf prefix-list {send | receive | both}.

syntax: neighbor <IP-ADDRESS> capability orf prefix-list {send | receive | both}

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the neighbor capability orf prefix-list command?
Syntax: neighbor <IP-ADDRESS> capability orf prefix-list {send | receive | both}

syntax: neighbor <IP-ADDRESS> capability orf prefix-list {send | receive | both}

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the neighbor default-originate command do?

The neighbor default-originate command enables the local router to send the default route 0.0.0.0 to a neighbor. The neighbor can then use this route to reach the router when all other routes are unavailable. Use the route-map option to configure the route map to modify the default route attributes. The no form of this command disables this feature. Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} default-originate [route-map <MAP-NAME>].
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the neighbor default-originate command?
Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} default-originate [route-map <MAP-NAME>]
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the neighbor ebgp-multihop command?
Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDR> | <PEER-GROUP-NAME>} ebgp-multihop <HOP-COUNT>
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the neighbor fall-over command do?

The neighbor fall-over command enables BGP fast peering session deactivation. When neighbor fall-over is configured, the BGP process monitors the RIB and if the route to peer is not present in the routing table, it immediately deactivates the peer session without waiting for the hold down timer. It is disabled by default. The no form of this command disables this feature. Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} fall-over.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the neighbor fall-over command?
Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} fall-over
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the neighbor fall-over bfd command do?

The neighbor fall-over bfd command enables BGP to register with BFD to receive fast peering session deactivation messages from BFD. You can either configure BFD support for BGP per neighbor or peer-group. The no form of this command disables BGP for BFD. NOTE: Multihop BFD is not supported for BGP. BFD over BGP using IPv6 is not supported on 8320 and 8325 switch series. Syntax: neighbor {<IPv4-ADDR>|<IPv6-ADDR>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} fall-over bfd.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the neighbor fall-over bfd command?
Syntax: neighbor {<IPv4-ADDR>|<IPv6-ADDR>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} fall-over bfd
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the neighbor graceful-shutdown command do?

The neighbor graceful-shutdown command configures the wait time before shutting down the BGP neighbor session, and can also configure the local preference value to be advertised before graceful shutdown. The no form of this command sets the wait time to the default value of 180 seconds and the local- preference value to the default of 0. Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDR> | <PEER-GROUP-NAME>} graceful-shutdown.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the neighbor graceful-shutdown command?
Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDR> | <PEER-GROUP-NAME>} graceful-shutdown
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the neighbor local-as command do?

The neighbor local-as command configures an alternate local AS number that can be used to establish a session with a peer, allowing a router to appear to be a member of a second autonomous system (AS), and its real AS. Local AS allows two autonomous systems to merge without modifying peering arrangements. This command is valid only for external peers. The no form of this command restores the default, which is for a peering session to be established using the primary AS (primary AS is the AS number specified at the time of neighbor creation using the command neighbor remote-as). Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} local-as <AS-NUMBER>.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the neighbor local-as command?
Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} local-as <AS-NUMBER>
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the neighbor next-hop-self command do?

The neighbor next-hop-self command configures the router as the next hop for a BGP-speaking neighbor or peer group, and enables BGP to send itself as the next hop for advertised routes. The no form of this command resets the peer nexthop-self status to default. The next hop will be generated based on the IP. Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} next-hop-self.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the neighbor next-hop-self command?
Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} next-hop-self
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the neighbor next-hop-unchanged command do?

The neighbor next-hop-unchanged command enables the neighbor to preserve next-hop while advertising routes to eBGP peers, in the L2VPN EVPN address-family. The no form of this command resets the peer next-hop-unchanged status to default. Syntax: neighbor <IP-ADDRESS> next-hop-unchanged.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the neighbor next-hop-unchanged command?
Syntax: neighbor <IP-ADDRESS> next-hop-unchanged
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the neighbor passive command do?

The neighbor passive command configures a specific neighbor, or all neighbors in a peer-group, as passive, which means that they will not initiate the TCP session. The neighbor connection must be reset using clear ip bgp for this setting to take effect. The no form of this command enables the neighbor to initiate the TCP session. Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} passive.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the neighbor passive command?
Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} passive
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the neighbor port command?
Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} port <NUMBER>
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the neighbor remote-as command do?

The neighbor remote-as command creates a peer, initiates the connection to the peer, and adds an entry to the BGP neighbor table. Specifies a neighbor with an autonomous system (AS) number that identifies the neighbor as internal to the local autonomous system. Otherwise, the neighbor is considered as external. By default, neighbors that are defined using this command, exchange only unicast address prefixes. The no form of this command disables the peer session and deletes the peer information. Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} remote-as <AS-NUMBER>.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the neighbor remote-as command?
Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} remote-as <AS-NUMBER>
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the neighbor remove-private-AS command?
Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDRESS>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} remove-private-AS
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the neighbor route-map command do?
The neighbor route-map command is documented in this guide. Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDRESS>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} route-map <MAP-NAME> {in|out}.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the neighbor route-map command?
Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDRESS>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} route-map <MAP-NAME> {in|out}
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the neighbor route-reflector-client command do?

The neighbor route-reflector-client command this command configures the router as a BGP route reflector and the specified peer as its client. The no form of this command disables this function. Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDRESS>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} route-reflector-client.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the neighbor route-reflector-client command?
Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDRESS>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} route-reflector-client
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the neighbor send-community command do?

The neighbor send-community command this command allows community values to be sent to a specific neighbor. When this command is configured for the peer-group, then all the neighbors that are part of peer-group will send the community values to the peers. The parameters standard and extended send only the respective community numbers. When the command is issued without either of these parameters, both standard and extended communities will be sent to the neighbor. The no form of this command will not allow the neighbor to send community values to the specific neighbors that are part of peer-group. Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDRESS>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} send-community [standard | extended].
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the neighbor send-community command?
Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDRESS>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} send-community [standard | extended]
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the neighbor shutdown command do?

The neighbor shutdown command this command disables the peer session, terminates any active session for the specified neighbor or peer group, and removes all associated routing information. This action can cause the sudden termination of many peering sessions. The no form of this command enables the peer session for the specified neighbor. Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDRESS>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} shutdown.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the neighbor shutdown command?
Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDRESS>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} shutdown
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the neighbor soft-reconfiguration inbound command do?

The neighbor soft-reconfiguration inbound command this command enables you to generate inbound updates from a neighbor and change and activate BGP policies without clearing the BGP session. Changes in BGP policies require the BGP session to be cleared which can have a large negative impact on network operations. The no form of this command disables this setting. Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDRESS>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} soft-reconfiguration inbound.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the neighbor soft-reconfiguration inbound command?
Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDRESS>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} soft-reconfiguration inbound
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the neighbor timers command do?

The neighbor timers command this command sets the timers for a specific BGP neighbor or peer group. When the timer is applied to peer- group then all the neighbors that are part of peer-group will inherit the value configured. 228. Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDRESS>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} timers <KEEPALIVE> <HOLDTIME>.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the neighbor timers command?
Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDRESS>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} timers <KEEPALIVE> <HOLDTIME>
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the neighbor ttl-security-hops command do?

The neighbor ttl-security-hops command this command enables BGP to establish connection with external peers residing on networks that are not directly connected. By enabling this feature, the received TTL from a BGP peer is compared with the difference "255 - hop-count". BGP messages coming with a TTL less than this value are not accepted. BGP peering will not be established if the TTL in the session establishment is received with a lower value. Also, by enabling this feature the router will send BGP packets with TTL value of 255 to the neighbor. For a neighbor, either TTL security or ebgp-multihop can be configured, not both together. If there are multiple paths to reach the node, then the hop count should be configured considering the longest route. The no form of this command disables the peer ttl-security-hop feature. Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDRESS> | <PEER-GROUP-NAME>} ttl-security-hops <HOP-COUNT>.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the neighbor ttl-security-hops command?
Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDRESS> | <PEER-GROUP-NAME>} ttl-security-hops <HOP-COUNT>
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the neighbor weight command do?

The neighbor weight command this command assigns a weight to a neighbor connection. When the weight is applied to a peer-group then all the neighbors that are part of the peer-group will inherit the value configured. The no form of this command removes a weight assignment. Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDRESS>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} weight <WEIGHT-VALUE>.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the neighbor weight command?
Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDRESS>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} weight <WEIGHT-VALUE>
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the network {[<IPv4/MASK> | <IPv6/MASK>]} [route-map <ROUTE-MAP-NAME>] command do?

The network {[<IPv4/MASK> | <IPv6/MASK>]} [route-map <ROUTE-MAP-NAME>] command this command specifies the networks to be advertised by the Border Gateway Protocol (BGP) routing processes. The no form of this command removes an entry from the routing table. Syntax: network {[<IPv4/MASK> | <IPv6/MASK>]} [route-map <ROUTE-MAP-NAME>].
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the network {[<IPv4/MASK> | <IPv6/MASK>]} [route-map <ROUTE-MAP-NAME>] command?
Syntax: network {[<IPv4/MASK> | <IPv6/MASK>]} [route-map <ROUTE-MAP-NAME>]
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the router bgp command do?

The router bgp command this command configures the BGP instance on the router, configures the AS (Autonomous System) the router belongs to, and enters into the BGP router configuration mode. Only a single BGP AS number can be assigned for the entire system. The no form of the command deletes the BGP instance from the router. Syntax: router bgp <AS-NUMBER>.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the router bgp command?
Syntax: router bgp <AS-NUMBER>

The show bgp flap-statistics command displays all the flapped and suppressed routes. 240. Syntax: show bgp {vrf <VRF_NAME> | all-vrf} [{IPv4 unicast | IPv6 unicast | all}] flap-statistics.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show running-config bgp command do?

For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the ip aspath-list command do?

The ip aspath-list command configures an AS Path list to match a specific AS path. AS Path lists are named lists of regular expression rules. They are used to match AS Path attributes in the routes for inclusion in or exclusion from route 268. Syntax: ip aspath-list <ASPATH-LIST-NAME> [seq <SEQ>] {permit | deny} <REGEXP>.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the ip aspath-list command?
Syntax: ip aspath-list <ASPATH-LIST-NAME> [seq <SEQ>] {permit | deny} <REGEXP>
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the ip prefix-list command?
Syntax: ip prefix-list <PREFIX-LIST-NAME> [seq <SEQ>] {ip | IPv6} <IP-PREFIX/MASK> [ge <0-128>] [le <0-128>]
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the route-map <NAME> {permit | deny} seq <NUMBER> command?
Syntax: route-map <NAME> {permit | deny} seq <NUMBER>
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the continue <SEQUENCE NUMBER> command do?

The continue <SEQUENCE NUMBER> command allows you to execute additional entries in a route map. The sequence number specifies the route-map entry's sequence number that will be executed next if the existing entry's match clause is successful. If a successful match occurs and continue command exists, the route map saves the set value first and then jumps to the specified route map entry. Set clauses are saved during the match clause evaluation and are executed only after the route map evaluation is completed. The set clauses are executed in the order in which they were configured. Set clauses can be accumulative or additive as set as-path prepend or it can be absolute as set metric. For set commands that configures an accumulative value, subsequent values are added in order in which they were configured. Syntax: continue <SEQUENCE NUMBER>.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the continue <SEQUENCE NUMBER> command?
Syntax: continue <SEQUENCE NUMBER>
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the match aspath-list <ASPATH-LIST-NAME> command do?

The match aspath-list <ASPATH-LIST-NAME> command matches the AS path attribute of the route with one or more regular expressions in the AS path list. The no form of this command restores the default behavior of not matching the AS path attribute of the route. Syntax: match aspath-list <ASPATH-LIST-NAME>.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the match aspath-list <ASPATH-LIST-NAME> command?
Syntax: match aspath-list <ASPATH-LIST-NAME>
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the match interface <INTERFACE-NAME> command do?

The match interface <INTERFACE-NAME> command matches the community number attribute of the route with one, or more regular expressions in the community-list. The no form of this command restores the default behavior of not matching the community number attribute of the route. Matches the outgoing interface value of the route with the value configured in the match clause. The no form of this command restores the default behavior of not matching the outgoing interface value of the route. Syntax: match interface <INTERFACE-NAME>.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the match interface <INTERFACE-NAME> command?
Syntax: match interface <INTERFACE-NAME>
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the match ip address prefix-list <PREFIX-LIST-NAME> command do?

The match ip address prefix-list <PREFIX-LIST-NAME> command matches the destination IP address prefix of the routes with one or more addresses in the prefix list. The no form of this command restores the default behavior of not matching the destination IP address prefix of the routes to their default value. Matches the next-hop address of the route with the configured address in the match clause. The no form of this command restores the default behavior of not matching the next-hop address of the route. Syntax: match ip address prefix-list <PREFIX-LIST-NAME>.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the match ip address prefix-list <PREFIX-LIST-NAME> command?
Syntax: match ip address prefix-list <PREFIX-LIST-NAME>
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the match ip route-source prefix-list <PREFIX-LIST-NAME> command do?

The match ip route-source prefix-list <PREFIX-LIST-NAME> command matches the IP address of the source of the route using IP prefix lists. The no form of this command restores the default behavior of not matching the IP address of the route. Syntax: match ip route-source prefix-list <PREFIX-LIST-NAME>.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the match ip route-source prefix-list <PREFIX-LIST-NAME> command?
Syntax: match ip route-source prefix-list <PREFIX-LIST-NAME>
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the match local-preference <VALUE> command do?

The match local-preference <VALUE> command matches the local preference value of the route with the value configured in the match clause. The no form of this command restores the default behavior of not matching the local preference value of the route. Matches the MED value of the route with the value configured in the match clause. The no form of this command restores the default behavior of not matching the MED value of the route. Syntax: match local-preference <VALUE>.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the match local-preference <VALUE> command?
Syntax: match local-preference <VALUE>
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the match origin {igp | egp | incomplete} command do?

The match origin {igp | egp | incomplete} command matches the route origin attribute of the route with route configured in the match clause. The no form of this command restores the default behavior of not matching the route origin attribute of the route. Matches the metric-type value of the OSPF external route with the value configured in the match clause. The no form of this command restores the default behavior of not matching the metric-type value of the OSPF external route. Syntax: match origin {igp | egp | incomplete}.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the match origin {igp | egp | incomplete} command?
Syntax: match origin {igp | egp | incomplete}
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the match source-protocol {bgp | connected | ospf | static} command do?

The match source-protocol {bgp | connected | ospf | static} command matches the source routing protocol value of the route with the value configured in the match clause. The no form of this command restores the default behavior of not matching the source routing protocol value of the route. Syntax: match source-protocol {bgp | connected | ospf | static}.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the match source-protocol {bgp | connected | ospf | static} command?
Syntax: match source-protocol {bgp | connected | ospf | static}
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the set as-path exclude <AS> command do?

The set as-path exclude <AS> command matches the tag value of the route with the one configured in the match clause. Applies to static routes that will be redistributed to ospfv2 and ospfv3 protocols. The no form of this command removes the tag value of the route. Removes all occurrences of the configured AS Path from the AS Path attribute of the route. The no form of this command restores the default behavior of not modifying the AS Path attribute list. Syntax: set as-path exclude <AS>.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the set as-path exclude <AS> command?
Syntax: set as-path exclude <AS>
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the set dampening {half-life <VALUE> reuse <VALUE> suppress <VALUE> max-suppress-time <VALUE>} command do?

The set dampening {half-life <VALUE> reuse <VALUE> suppress <VALUE> max-suppress-time <VALUE>} command sets parameters of route flap dampening feature. Syntax: set dampening {half-life <VALUE> reuse <VALUE> suppress <VALUE> max-suppress-time <VALUE>}.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the set dampening {half-life <VALUE> reuse <VALUE> suppress <VALUE> max-suppress-time <VALUE>} command?
Syntax: set dampening {half-life <VALUE> reuse <VALUE> suppress <VALUE> max-suppress-time <VALUE>}
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the set {ip | IPv6} nexthop {global} <IP-ADDR> command do?

The set {ip | IPv6} nexthop {global} <IP-ADDR> command sets the IP address of the next-hop of the route with the value configured in the set clause. The no form of this command restores the default behavior of not modifying the IP address of the next-hop of the route. Modifies the local-preference attribute of the route entry with the value configured in the set clause. The no form of this command restores the default behavior of not modifying the local-preference attribute of the route entry. Syntax: set {ip | IPv6} nexthop {global} <IP-ADDR>.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the set {ip | IPv6} nexthop {global} <IP-ADDR> command?

For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the set metric <VALUE> command do?

The set metric <VALUE> command modifies the metric value of the route with the value configured in the set clause. The no form of this command restores the default behavior of not modifying the metric value of the route. Modifies the route origin attribute of the route update with the value configured in the set clause. The no form of this command restores the default behavior of not modifying the route origin attribute of the route. 284. Syntax: set metric <VALUE>.

For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the set tag <value> command do?

The set tag <value> command modifies the tag value of the route with the one configured in the set clause. Applicable to static routes that will be redistributed to ospfv2 and ospfv3 protocols. The no form of this command removes the set clause tag value. Syntax: set tag <value>.

Syntax: set tag <value>

The show ip aspath-list command shows the configuration details of the AS path list. Syntax: show ip aspath-list [<NAME>] [vsx-peer].

The show ip ecmp command displays the Equal Cost Multipath (ECMP) configuration. Syntax: show ip ecmp [vsx-peer].

For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what restrictions or notes are documented for Guidelines and limitations?

For Guidelines and limitations, the guide documents the following restrictions, notes, or considerations: • VRRP must be enabled at the global level using the router vrrp enable command. It must also be enabled at the interface level at which you configure VRRP. • A maximum of 8 IPv4 and 8 IPv6 VRRP groups are supported on an interface. • A maximum of 256 VRRP groups is supported on a router. The groups can be IPv4 or IPv6. • Supported virtual router identification (VRID) range is 1-255. A maximum of 8 VRIDs can be used to achieve a scale of 256 VRRP groups by using the same VRID across different interfaces. • Proxy-ARP and Active-Gateway are not supported in conjunction with VRRP.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the address <IP-ADDR> [ primary | secondary ] command do?

The address <IP-ADDR> [ primary | secondary ] command configures a primary or secondary IPv4 or IPv6 address for the VRRP group. To use secondary IP addresses in a VRRP group, you must first configure a primary IP address on the same group. A maximum of 16 IP addresses per IPv4 VRRP group and 8 IPv6 addresses per IPv6 VRRP group are supported. NOTE: Do not configure an IPv4 VRRP group using addresses from the /30, /31, and /32 subnets of the interface IP address. NOTE: 16 Virtual IP addresses per IPv4 VR and 8 Virtual IP addresses per IPv6 VR are supported. The total number of VIP s supported by a switch is: • 1024 VIP s for IPv4 VRs • 512 VIP s for IPv6 VRs The no form of this command deletes a primary or secondary IPv4 or IPv6 address from the VRRP group. Syntax: address <IP-ADDR> [ primary | secondary ].
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the address <IP-ADDR> [ primary | secondary ] command?
Syntax: address <IP-ADDR> [ primary | secondary ]
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the authentication {<text> | <md5>} {<plaintext> | <ciphertext>} <key-string> command?
Syntax: authentication {<text> | <md5>} {<plaintext> | <ciphertext>} <key-string>
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the preempt command do?

The preempt command enables the preempt option. The default value is enabled. In default mode, a backup router with a higher priority than another backup that is operating as master will take over the master function. Applies to VRRP backup routers only and is used to minimize network disruption caused by unnecessary preemption of the master operation among backup routers. The no form of this command disables the preempt option, thus preventing the higher-priority backup from taking over the master operation from a lower-priority backup. This command does not prevent an owner router from resuming the master function after recovering from being unavailable. Syntax: preempt.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the preempt command?
Syntax: preempt
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the preempt delay minimum <DELAY-IN-SECONDS> command do?

The preempt delay minimum <DELAY-IN-SECONDS> command sets the time in seconds (1-3600) that the router will wait before taking control of the virtual IP and starting to route packets. The no form of this command sets the preempt delay for the VRRP group to the default preempt delay of 0 seconds. The VRRP Preempt Delay Timer (PDT) allows admin users to configure a period of time before the VR takes control of the virtual IP address. It does not transition to the Master state until the timer period expires. The timer value configured should be long enough to allow upper layer protocol to converge. The PDT is applied only during initialization of the router. Sets the priority for the VRRP group. 302. Syntax: preempt delay minimum <DELAY-IN-SECONDS>.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the preempt delay minimum <DELAY-IN-SECONDS> command?
Syntax: preempt delay minimum <DELAY-IN-SECONDS>

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the track OBJECT-ID command do?

The track OBJECT-ID command sets the track object ID (1-128) for the group. The track object is first configured globally for the interface and then attached to the VRRP virtual router. NOTE: The track object must not track the same interface for which a VRRP group is configured. The no form of this command removes the track object ID from the group. Syntax: track OBJECT-ID.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the track OBJECT-ID command?
Syntax: track OBJECT-ID
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the track <OBJECT-ID> command do?

The track <OBJECT-ID> command configures a track object that can be associated with an interface. A change in interface state will then affect the priority of a VRRP group. By default, no interface is associated to a track object, so state is down. The no form of this command deletes a tracked object for an interface. If it is not associated with a VRRP virtual router, a track object cannot be deleted. NOTE: Track cannot be configured by using port with no routing. Specifies an interface to be tracked when changes in the state of the interface affect the priority of a VRRP group. Once track is associated with an interface, the track state reflects the interface forwarding state. The no form of this command removes an interface from tracking, affecting VRRP states of any interfaces associated with VRRP groups. Syntax: track <OBJECT-ID>.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the track <OBJECT-ID> command?
Syntax: track <OBJECT-ID>
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the version <VERSION-NUMBER> command do?

The version <VERSION-NUMBER> command sets the protocol version for the VRRP group. Version change is allowed only for the IPv4 address-family. The default value is 2, which supports IPv4 with minimum 1 second advertisement interval. Value 3 supports IPv4 and IPv6 with minimum 1 second advertisement interval. Syntax: version <VERSION-NUMBER>.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the version <VERSION-NUMBER> command?

For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what routing behavior is documented for Virtual Routing and Forwarding overview?

Virtual Routing and Forwarding: Virtual Routing and Forwarding (VRF) allows a physical router to be partitioned into multiple Virtual Router Forwarding instances (VRDs). The control and data plane are isolated in each VRF. This isolation keeps the traffic from crossing VRFs therefore multiple different routing tables can coexist within the same physical L3 switch/router. Route leaking allows for routes to “leak” or be distributed, across multiple VRFs in a controlled manner. Two methods to leak routes from one VRF to another are: • Static Routes • MP-BGP (or Dynamic Route leaking using route-targets). Static VRF route leak Static VRF route leaking between two VRFs is allowed using an IP route configuration command. To accomplish the route leak: • The routes being leaked to other VRFs must be present in the source VRF. • When a route leak has been accomplished and is therefore present in the source VRF, that route can then be leaked to other VRFs. Dynamic VRF route leak Dynamic VRF route leaking is allowed between two or more user configured VRFs.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the IPv6 route source interface command do?

The IPv6 route source interface command creates a route leak between the source VRF and destination VRF. Using the static method, the route must first be added to the destination VRF. The route is added to the local interface of the source VRF with a next- hop interface. The existing IPv6 route command takes the source interface only when next-hop IP is link- local. To support VRF route leaking for global IPv6 unicast addresses, the command takes next-hop interface information along with next-hop IP regardless of next-hop IP is link-local or not. • Users must provide both the next-hop IP and the interface information to leak the global unicast IPv6 network routes (route that is not directly reachable). • The next-hop IP information is not required to leak connected global unicast IPv6 routes (route that is directly reachable). Syntax: IPv6 route <IPv6 ADDR/MEMBER><IPv6 ADDR> source <INTERFACE>.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the IPv6 route source interface command?
Syntax: IPv6 route <IPv6 ADDR/MEMBER><IPv6 ADDR> source <INTERFACE>
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the ip route interface command do?

The ip route interface command creates a route leak between the SRC-VRF and DST-VRF. Using the static method, the route must first be added to the destination VRF. The route is added to the local interface of the source VRF with a next-hop interface. The existing IP route command can then take either the next-hop IP or the next-hop interface. 322. Syntax: ip route <IPv4 ADDR/MEMBER> interface <IPv4 ADDR>.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the ip route interface command?
Syntax: ip route <IPv4 ADDR/MEMBER> interface <IPv4 ADDR>

The route-target [import | export | both] <AS-NUMBER:NN> command the route-target [import | export | both] <AS-NUMBER: NN> command bGP route targets are extended BGP communities that identify the VPNv4 or VPNv6 routes that are associated with a VRF. This command specifies the route targets used on the import or export of the routes to other VRF s. Multiple route targets can be associated to a VRF. The no form of the command removes the association. Syntax: route-target [import | export | both] <AS-NUMBER:NN>.

For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what routing behavior is documented for PBR, VSX, and VLAN ACLs?

PBR, VSX, and VLAN ACLs: Multichassis Link Aggregation Group (MCLAG) with VSX is a high-availability feature where a switch containing LAG members is connected to multiple switches to allow for node-level redundancy on that link. If one of the other switches goes down the LAG remains up and can continue to carry all the LAG traffic, bandwidth permitting. A LAG (and an MCLAG) can be a member of a VLAN and a Layer 3 VSI (virtual switch interface), which can be created for that VLAN for the purposes of routing a policy with Layer 3 specific actions (that is, PBR), which can therefore be applied to that interface to influence routing decisions for matching Layer 3 packets on the MCLAG; like any other route-only port or VSI. Under such a configuration, it is likely that it is desirable to use VLAN ACLs applied to the VLAN the MCLAG is a member of. NOTE: There is a limit with this particular combination when the VLAN ACL specifies both IPv4 and IPv6 entries and the PBR policy has entries with IPv4 and IPv6 classes. This configuration will exhaust the switching ASIC resources and will fail to apply.

section: PBR software path, VSX, and VRRP

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what routing behavior is documented for PBR software path, VSX, and VRRP?

PBR software path, VSX, and VRRP: Due to the dynamic nature of the VSX and VRRP protocols, applying a policy with PBR to VSX or VRRP Layer 3 kernel interfaces and then making further changes to those protocols may not result in expected behavior. Apply policies with PBR to VSX and VRRP Layer 3 interfaces after all changes have been made. If further changes are necessary the policy should be removed first and reapplied when configuration updates are complete. PBR and next-hop router reachability When a policy with PBR actions is applied to an interface, all next-hop and default-nexthop entries in the associated action list are continuously monitored for reachability. Probing occurs every 5 seconds for each such entry and guarantees that the reaction to the loss of reachability, or detection of the new reachability of any such entry will be approximately 5 seconds.

topic: PBR software path, VSX, and VRRP

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what restrictions or notes are documented for Restrictions?

For Restrictions, the guide documents the following restrictions, notes, or considerations: The reachability of the next-hop routers/tunnel interfaces in the list is not guaranteed. Such reachability can change at any time due to the dynamic nature of the network environment. Usage Each action list may contain up to eight entries of four different entry types: • interface null • interface tunnel • nexthop • default-nexthop List entries have a unique sequence number which, if not user specified, are automatically assigned beginning at 10 and continuing at intervals of 10 for each subsequent new list entry, for example 20, 30, and 40. Sequence numbers of any value can be specified manually, a different interval may be set, and new entries can be added to (or removed from) any location in the list at any time. Specifying an existing sequence number causes the existing list entry to be replaced by the new details. The list entry with the lowest sequence number has the highest priority entry in the list. The sequence numbers may be renumbered with the pbr-action-list resequence command. 344.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the pbr-action-list <ACTION-LIST-NAME> copy <DESTINATION-ACTION-LIST-NAME> command do?
The pbr-action-list <ACTION-LIST-NAME> copy <DESTINATION-ACTION-LIST-NAME> command copies an existing PBR action list. Syntax: pbr-action-list <ACTION-LIST-NAME> copy <DESTINATION-ACTION-LIST-NAME>.

The pbr-action-list <ACTION-LIST-NAME> resequence <STARTING-SEQUENCE-NUMBER> <INCREMENT> command renumbers the entries in an action list. The list entry with the lowest sequence number has the highest priority entry in the list. Syntax: pbr-action-list <ACTION-LIST-NAME> resequence <STARTING-SEQUENCE-NUMBER> <INCREMENT>.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the client track ip { enable | disable | auto } command do?

The client track ip { enable | disable | auto } command enables client IP address tracking on the specified set of interfaces. Tracking will take effect only if client IP address tracking is enabled at both the system level and for the VLAN to which the port belongs. Default: auto. The no form of the command disables client IP address tracking on the specified set of interfaces. Syntax: client track ip { enable | disable | auto }.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the router rip command do?

The router rip command creates RIP process if not already created and enters the router rip <PROCESS-ID> context for the VRF mentioned. If no VRF is mentioned, a default is used. Only one RIP process is allowed per VRF. The no form of this command deletes the RIP instance for the VRF. If no VRF is mentioned the default is deleted. 376. Syntax: router rip <PROCESS-ID> [vrf <VRF-NAME>].

The redistribute {bgp | connected | ospf <PROCESS-ID> | static} command sets the maximum number of ECMP routes that RIP can support. The no form of this command sets the maximum number of ECMP routes to the default value of 4. Redistributes routes originating from other protocols into RIP. The no form of this command disables redistribution of routes originating from other protocols into RIP. Syntax: redistribute {bgp | connected | ospf <PROCESS-ID> | static}.

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the router ripng command do?

The router ripng command creates RIPng process if not already created and enters the router ripng <PROCESS-ID> context for the VRF mentioned. If no VRF is mentioned, a default is used. Only one RIPng process is allowed per VRF. The no form of this command deletes the RIPng instance for the VRF. If no VRF is mentioned the default is deleted. Syntax: router ripng <PROCESS-ID> [vrf <VRF-NAME>].

messages.content: For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is IRDP?

IRDP: ICMP Router Discovery Protocol (IRDP), an extension of the ICMP, is independent of any routing protocol. It allows hosts to discover the IP addresses of neighboring routers that can act as default gateways to reach devices on other IP networks. IRDP operation IRDP uses the following types of ICMP messages: • Router advertisement (RA): Sent by a router to advertise IP addresses (including the primary and secondary IP addresses) and preference. • Router solicitation (RS): Sent by a host to request the IP addresses of routers on the subnet. An interface with IRDP enabled periodically broadcasts or multicasts an RA message to advertise its IP addresses. A receiving host adds the IP addresses to its routing table, and selects the IP address with the highest preference as the default gateway. When a host attached to the subnet starts up, the host multicasts an RS message to request immediate advertisements. If the host does not receive any advertisements, it retransmits the RS several times.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the diag-dump irdp basic command do?
The diag-dump irdp basic command displays diagnostic information for IRDP. Syntax: diag-dump irdp basic.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the diag-dump irdp basic command?
Syntax: diag-dump irdp basic
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the ip irdp command do?

The ip irdp command enables IRDP on an interface and specifies the packet type that is used to send advertisements. By default, the packet type is set to multicast. IRDP is only supported on layer 3 interfaces. The no form of this command disables IRDP on an interface. Syntax: ip irdp [broadcast | multicast].
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the ip irdp command?
Syntax: ip irdp [broadcast | multicast]
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the ip irdp holdtime command do?

The ip irdp holdtime command specifies the maximum amount of time the host will consider an advertisement to be valid until a newer advertisement arrives. When a new advertisement arrives, hold time is reset. Hold time must be greater than or equal to the maximum advertisement interval. Therefore, if the hold time for an advertisement 14 AOS-CX 10.06 IP Services Guide expires, the host can reasonably conclude that the router interface that sent the advertisement is no longer available. The default hold time is three times the maximum advertisement interval. Syntax: ip irdp holdtime <TIME>.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the ip irdp holdtime command?
Syntax: ip irdp holdtime <TIME>
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the ip irdp maxadvertinterval command do?
The ip irdp maxadvertinterval command specifies the maximum router advertisement interval. Syntax: ip irdp maxadvertinterval <TIME>.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the ip irdp maxadvertinterval command?
Syntax: ip irdp maxadvertinterval <TIME>
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the ip irdp minadvertinterval command do?

The ip irdp minadvertinterval command specifies the minimum amount of time the switch waits between sending router advertisements. By default, this value is automatically set by the switch to be 75% of the value configured for maximum router advertisement interval. Use this command to override the automatically configured value. Syntax: ip irdp minadvertinterval <TIME>.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the ip irdp minadvertinterval command?
Syntax: ip irdp minadvertinterval <TIME>
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the ip irdp preference command do?

The ip irdp preference command specifies the IRDP preference level. If a host receives multiple router advertisement messages from different routers, the host selects the router that sent the message with the highest preference as the default gateway. Syntax: ip irdp preference <LEVEL>.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the ip irdp preference command?
Syntax: ip irdp preference <LEVEL>
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show ip irdp command do?
The show ip irdp command displays IRDP configuration settings. Syntax: show ip irdp [vsx-peer].
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show ip irdp command?
Syntax: show ip irdp [vsx-peer]
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the guide say about IPv6 Router Advertisement?

IPv6 Router Advertisement: IPv6 RA provides a method for local IPv6 hosts to automatically configure their own IP address (and other settings such as a preferred DNS server) based on information advertised by switches/routers operating on the network. IPv6 flags Behavior of IPv6 hosts to IPv6 RA messages is controlled by the managed address configuration flag (M flag), and other stateful configuration flag (O flag). M flag O flag Description 0 0 1 1 0 1 0 1 Indicates that no information is available via DHCPv6. Indicates that other configuration information is available via DHCPv6. Examples of such information are DNS-related information or information on other servers within the network. Indicates that addresses are available via Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCPv6). If the M flag is set, the O flag is redundant and can be ignored because DHCPv6 will return all available configuration information.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, how do you configure IPv6 RA?

To configure IPv6 RA, follow the documented procedure: 1. Enable transmission of IPv6 router advertisements with the command no IPv6 nd suppress-ra 2. Optionally, configure IPv6 unicast address prefixes with the command IPv6 nd prefix 3. Optionally, configure support for DNS name resolution with the commands IPv6 nd ra dns server.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the IPv6 address autoconfig command do?

The IPv6 address autoconfig command enables the interface to automatically obtain an IPv6 address using router advertisement information and the EUI-64 identifier. The no form of this command disables address auto-configuration. Syntax: IPv6 address autoconfig.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the IPv6 address autoconfig command?
Syntax: IPv6 address autoconfig
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the IPv6 address link-local command do?

The IPv6 address link-local command enables IPv6 on the current interface. If no address is specified, an IPv6 link-local address is auto-generated for the interface. If an address is specified, auto-configuration is disabled and the specified address/mask is assigned to the interface. To disable IPv6 link-local on the interface, remove IPv6 address link-local, IPv6 address <global- IPv6-address>, and IPv6 address autoconfig from the interface. NOTE: This feature is not available on the management VRF. Syntax: IPv6 address link-local [<IPv6-ADDR>/<MASK>].
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the IPv6 address link-local command?
Syntax: IPv6 address link-local [<IPv6-ADDR>/<MASK>]
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the IPv6 nd cache-limit command do?

The IPv6 nd cache-limit command configures the limit on the number of neighbor entries in the ND cache. The no form of this command sets the cache limit to the default value. Syntax: IPv6 nd cache-limit <CACHELIMIT>.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the IPv6 nd cache-limit command?
Syntax: IPv6 nd cache-limit <CACHELIMIT>
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the IPv6 nd dad attempts command do?

The IPv6 nd dad attempts command configures the number of neighbor solicitations to be sent when performing duplicate address detection (DAD) for a unicast address configured on an interface. The no form of this command sets the number of attempts to the default value. Syntax: IPv6 nd dad attempts <NUM-ATTEMPTS>.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the IPv6 nd dad attempts command?
Syntax: IPv6 nd dad attempts <NUM-ATTEMPTS>
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the IPv6 nd hop-limit command do?

The IPv6 nd hop-limit command configures the hop limit to be sent in RAs. The no form of this command resets the hop limit to 0. This reset eliminates the hop limit from the RAs that originate on the interface, so the host determines the hop limit. Syntax: IPv6 nd hop-limit <HOPLIMIT>.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the IPv6 nd hop-limit command?
Syntax: IPv6 nd hop-limit <HOPLIMIT>
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the IPv6 nd mtu command do?
The IPv6 nd mtu command configures the MTU size to be sent in the RA messages. The no form of this command sets hop limit to the default value. Syntax: IPv6 nd mtu <MTU-VALUE>.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the IPv6 nd mtu command?
Syntax: IPv6 nd mtu <MTU-VALUE>
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the IPv6 nd ns-interval command do?

The IPv6 nd ns-interval command configures the ND time between DAD neighbor solicitations sent for an unresolved destination, or between duplicate address detection neighbor solicitation requests. Increase this setting when neighbor solicitation retries or failures are occurring, or in a slow (WAN) network. The no form of this command sets the ns-interval to the default value. Syntax: IPv6 nd ns-interval <TIME>.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the IPv6 nd ns-interval command?
Syntax: IPv6 nd ns-interval <TIME>
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the IPv6 nd prefix command do?

The IPv6 nd prefix command specifies prefixes for the routing switch to include in RAs transmitted on the interface. IPv6 hosts use the prefixes in RAs to autoconfigure themselves with global unicast addresses. The autoconfigured address of a host is composed of the advertised prefix and the interface identifier in the current link-local address of the host. By default, advertise, autoconfig, and onlink are set. The no form of this command removes the configuration on the interface. Syntax: IPv6 nd prefix <IPv6-ADDR>/<PREFIX-LEN>.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the IPv6 nd prefix command?
Syntax: IPv6 nd prefix <IPv6-ADDR>/<PREFIX-LEN>
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the IPv6 nd ra dns search-list command do?

The IPv6 nd ra dns search-list command configures the DNS Search List (DNSSL) to include in Router Advertisements (RAs) transmitted on the interface. The no form of this command removes the DNS Search List from the RAs transmitted on the interface. Syntax: IPv6 nd ra dns search-list <DOMAIN-NAME> [lifetime <TIME>].
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the IPv6 nd ra dns search-list command?
Syntax: IPv6 nd ra dns search-list <DOMAIN-NAME> [lifetime <TIME>]
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the IPv6 nd ra dns server command do?

The IPv6 nd ra dns server command configures the IPv6 address of a preferred Recursive DNS Server (RDNSS) to be included in Router Advertisements (RAs) transmitted on the interface. The no form of this command removes the configured DNS server from the RAs transmitted on the interface. Syntax: IPv6 nd ra dns server <IPv6-ADDR> [lifetime <TIME>].
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the IPv6 nd ra dns server command?
Syntax: IPv6 nd ra dns server <IPv6-ADDR> [lifetime <TIME>]
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the IPv6 nd ra lifetime command do?

The IPv6 nd ra lifetime command configures the lifetime, in seconds, for the routing switch to be used as a default router by hosts on the current interface. The no form of this command sets lifetime to the default of 1800 seconds. Syntax: IPv6 nd ra lifetime <TIME>.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the IPv6 nd ra lifetime command?
Syntax: IPv6 nd ra lifetime <TIME>
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the IPv6 nd ra managed-config-flag command do?

The IPv6 nd ra managed-config-flag command controls the M fLAGsetting in RAs the router transmits on the current interface. Enable the M flag to indicate that hosts can obtain IP address through DHCPv6. The M flag is disabled by default. The no form of this command turns off (disables) the M flag. Syntax: IPv6 nd ra managed-config-flag.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the IPv6 nd ra managed-config-flag command?
Syntax: IPv6 nd ra managed-config-flag
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the IPv6 nd ra max-interval command do?

The IPv6 nd ra max-interval command configures the maximum interval between transmissions of IPv6 RAs on the interface. The interval between RA transmissions on an interface is a random value that changes every time an RA is sent. The interval is calculated to be a value between the current max-interval and min-interval settings. The no form of this command returns the setting to its default, provided the default value is less than the default lifetime value. Syntax: IPv6 nd ra max-interval <TIME>.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the IPv6 nd ra max-interval command?
Syntax: IPv6 nd ra max-interval <TIME>
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the IPv6 nd ra min-interval command do?

The IPv6 nd ra min-interval command configures the minimum interval between transmissions of IPv6 RAs on the interface. The interval between RA transmissions on an interface is a random value that changes every time an RA is sent. The interval is calculated to be a value between the current max-interval and min-interval settings. The no form of this command returns the setting to its default, provided the default value is less than the current max-interval setting. Syntax: IPv6 nd ra min-interval <TIME>.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the IPv6 nd ra min-interval command?
Syntax: IPv6 nd ra min-interval <TIME>
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the IPv6 nd ra other-config-flag command do?

The IPv6 nd ra other-config-flag command controls the O-bit in RAs the router transmits on the current interface; but is ignored unless the M-bit is disabled in RAs. Configure to set the O-bit in RA messages for host to obtain network parameters through DHCPv6. The other-config-flag is disabled by default. For more information on configuring the M-bit, see IPv6 nd ra managed-config-flag. The no form of this command turns off (disables) the setting for this command in RAs. Syntax: IPv6 nd ra other-config-flag.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the IPv6 nd ra other-config-flag command?
Syntax: IPv6 nd ra other-config-flag
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the IPv6 nd ra reachable-time command do?

The IPv6 nd ra reachable-time command sets the amount of time that the interface considers a device to be reachable after receiving a reachability confirmation from the device. The no form of this command sets the reachable time to the default value of 0. (no limit). Syntax: IPv6 nd ra reachable-time <TIME>.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the IPv6 nd ra reachable-time command?
Syntax: IPv6 nd ra reachable-time <TIME>
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the IPv6 nd ra retrans-timer command do?

The IPv6 nd ra retrans-timer command configures the period (retransmit timer) between ND solicitations sent by a host for an unresolved destination, or between DAD neighbor solicitation requests. By default, hosts on the interface use their own locally configured NS-interval settings instead of using the value received in the RAs. Increase this timer when neighbor solicitation retries or failures are occur, or in a "slow" (WAN) network. The no form of this command sets the value to the default of 0. Syntax: IPv6 nd ra retrans-timer <TIME>.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the IPv6 nd ra retrans-timer command?
Syntax: IPv6 nd ra retrans-timer <TIME>
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the IPv6 nd router-preference command do?

The IPv6 nd router-preference command specifies the value that is set in the Default Router Preference (DRP) field of Router Advertisements (RAs) that the switch sends from an interface. An interface with a DRP value of high will be preferred by other devices on the network over interfaces with an RA value of medium or low. The no form of this command set the value to the default of medium. Syntax: IPv6 nd router-preference {high | medium | low}.
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the IPv6 nd router-preference command?
Syntax: IPv6 nd router-preference {high | medium | low}
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the IPv6 nd suppress-ra command do?

The IPv6 nd suppress-ra command configures suppression of IPv6 Router Advertisement transmissions on an interface. 34 AOS-CX 10.06 IP Services Guide The no form of this command restores transmission of IPv6 Router Advertisement and options. Syntax: IPv6 nd suppress-ra [<SUPPRESS-OPTION>].
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the IPv6 nd suppress-ra command?
Syntax: IPv6 nd suppress-ra [<SUPPRESS-OPTION>]
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show IPv6 nd global traffic command do?
The show IPv6 nd global traffic command displays IPv6 Neighbor Discovery traffic details on a device. Syntax: show IPv6 nd global traffic [vsx-peer].
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show IPv6 nd global traffic command?
Syntax: show IPv6 nd global traffic [vsx-peer]
For 6300_6400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show IPv6 nd interface command do?

The show IPv6 nd interface command displays neighbor discovery information for an interface. If no options are specified, displays information for the default VRF. Syntax: show IPv6 nd interface [<IF-NAME> | all-vrfs | vrf <VRF-NAME>].

syntax: show IPv6 nd interface [<IF-NAME> | all-vrfs | vrf <VRF-NAME>]

Syntax: show IPv6 nd interface [<IF-NAME> | all-vrfs | vrf <VRF-NAME>]

The show IPv6 nd interface prefix command shows IPv6 prefix information for all VRFs or a specific VRF. If no options are specified, shows information for the default VRF. Syntax: show IPv6 nd interface prefix [all-vrfs | vrf <VRF-NAME>] [vsx-peer].
